title: FAANG Engineering Facts
description: Real scale numbers, architectural decisions, and engineering culture facts from Meta, Amazon, Apple, Netflix, and Google. These are the facts that make system design discussions grounded in reality.

sections.title: Meta (Facebook) — Social Graph at Planetary Scale
sections.description:
META
3.3B
Daily Active Users (DAU)
>1M
Servers in datacenters
100TB+
Data ingested per day
800M+
WhatsApp messages/day
TAO — The Association/Object Database. Meta built a custom distributed graph database called TAO (The Associations and Objects) to power the social graph. It sits above MySQL, using a read-heavy caching layer across 1000s of servers. Every "friend of a friend" query goes through TAO.

RocksDB. Meta open-sourced RocksDB (LSM-tree KV store) in 2013 — now used everywhere (CockroachDB, TiKV, Kafka, LinkedIn). It replaced LevelDB for high write throughput workloads.

Cassandra. Originally created at Facebook for inbox search in 2007, donated to Apache. Designed for write-heavy, geographically distributed, eventual-consistency workloads (leaderless replication).

Hack / PHP at scale. Facebook ran one of the largest PHP codebases in the world. They built HHVM (JIT compiler for PHP/Hack) and later Hack, a gradually-typed PHP dialect — proving you don't need to rewrite a 50M LOC codebase to get performance.

React. Open-sourced in 2013. Now the most-used frontend framework. Built to solve the "cascading update" problem in Facebook's News Feed — reconciling a complex UI tree efficiently.

News Feed ranking. Evaluated ~1500 signals per post. Uses a multi-stage pipeline: candidate generation (EdgeRank era) → lightweight ML scoring → heavy NN ranking → policy filtering → diversity sampling.

sections.title: Amazon — The Everything Store's Infrastructure
sections.description:
AMAZON
$100B+
AWS annual revenue (2023)
33%
Global cloud market share
500ms
Latency = -1% revenue (measured)
1.5M+
Deployments per year (circa 2014)
Two-Pizza Teams & SOA (2002 Bezos Mandate). Jeff Bezos issued the famous memo: all teams must communicate through service interfaces, no exceptions. No direct DB access between teams. Teams must build APIs as if they'd sell them to external developers. This mandate created AWS — they productized their internal infrastructure.

Dynamo (2007). Amazon's internal highly-available KV store. The paper introduced: consistent hashing with virtual nodes, sloppy quorum (W+R>N), vector clocks for conflict resolution, anti-entropy via Merkle trees, gossip protocol for membership. Apache Cassandra and Riak are direct descendants.

Aurora. Custom MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible cloud DB. Key innovation: separates storage from compute. A 6-way replicated distributed storage layer (2 copies in 3 AZs). Only redo logs are sent over the network — not full data pages. This reduces network I/O by 7.7×.

Graviton chips. Amazon designed their own Arm-based CPUs (AWS Graviton). Graviton3 delivers up to 25% better compute performance, 60% lower energy versus comparable x86. This is now 40%+ of new EC2 deployments.

Fulfillment at scale. Amazon operates 1000+ fulfillment centers. Their warehouse robotics (Kiva, acquired 2012) drive goods to pickers rather than pickers walking to goods — cutting walking distance from 10–20km/day to near-zero. Same-day delivery now operates in 90+ metro areas.

Prime Video monolith (2023). Famously moved from microservices back to a monolith for a specific video monitoring service. Cut infrastructure costs by 90%, reduced latency. Clarified that "monolith vs microservices" is always a trade-off, not a universal truth.

sections.title: Apple — Hardware + Software Vertical Integration
sections.description:
APPLE
$383B
Revenue FY2023
2.2B
Active devices worldwide
3nm
M3 chip process node
~1B
iCloud users
Apple Silicon (M-series). The move from Intel x86 to ARM-based Apple Silicon (M1, 2020) was a 2-year secret project. Key engineering decisions: unified memory architecture (CPU, GPU, Neural Engine share one memory pool — no VRAM copying), custom ISA extensions, hardware ML accelerators. M3 Max reaches 14.2 TFLOPS GPU, 18 TFLOPS Neural Engine.

Secure Enclave. A dedicated coprocessor in every Apple device with its own isolated memory. Stores cryptographic keys, processes biometric data, handles Apple Pay transactions. Never exposed to the main CPU's address space — not even Apple can access it. This is the gold standard of consumer hardware security.

iCloud at scale. 2B+ active devices syncing to iCloud. Uses multiple storage backends: CloudKit (structured data/metadata), S3-compatible object storage for blobs, CRDTs for conflict-free offline editing (Notes, Reminders). CRDTs allow concurrent edits that merge deterministically without a central server round-trip.

App Store review pipeline. ~10,000 app submissions per day, reviewed in 24–48 hours. Automated static analysis catches 90%+ of issues (malware, API misuse, sandbox violations). Human review handles policy edge cases. Built custom binary analysis tooling at scale (no open-source frameworks).

Swift vs Objective-C. Apple rewrote Swift from scratch (Chris Lattner, 2010–2014) to fix ObjC's unsafe memory model, verbose syntax, and ABI instability. Swift now compiles to LLVM IR, supports copy-on-write semantics for value types, and has a memory model designed to eliminate entire classes of bugs (dangling pointers, buffer overflows).

sections.title: Netflix — Streaming at Internet Scale
sections.description:
NETFLIX
238M+
Paid subscribers worldwide
15%
of global internet bandwidth
700+
CDN edge locations (Open Connect)
2M+
Data pipeline tasks/day
Open Connect CDN. Netflix built its own CDN by placing dedicated appliances (Open Connect Appliances — OCAs) inside ISP networks. ISPs get free appliances; Netflix gets cheaper delivery. Pre-loads popular content during off-peak hours via proactive caching. 95%+ of traffic served from OCAs, not AWS.

Chaos Engineering (Chaos Monkey, 2011). Netflix invented chaos engineering by randomly killing production servers to force engineers to build resilient systems. Expanded to the "Simian Army": Chaos Gorilla (kills AZs), Chaos Kong (kills regions), Latency Monkey (adds latency). Netflix now has a dedicated Chaos Engineering team.

Hystrix / Circuit Breaker. Open-sourced Netflix's internal library for latency and fault tolerance. Implements the circuit breaker pattern: after a threshold of failures, stop calling the downstream service and return a fallback. Prevents cascading failures in microservice systems.

Recommendation at scale. Netflix's recommendation system serves ~1B personalized requests/day. Uses matrix factorization (ALS), bandit algorithms for exploration, and A/B testing infrastructure that runs 250+ simultaneous experiments. The recommendation engine drives 80% of content watched.

Content encoding. Netflix encodes each title at ~100 different bitrate/resolution/codec combinations (H.264, H.265/HEVC, AV1, VP9). They use per-title encoding: complex scenes use higher bitrate, simple scenes use lower — cutting bandwidth 20% while maintaining quality. AV1 (royalty-free) is their long-term codec bet.

Microservices pioneer. Netflix was an early, high-profile adopter of the microservices pattern at scale. They open-sourced most of their stack: Eureka (service discovery), Ribbon (client-side load balancing), Zuul (API gateway), Hystrix (circuit breaker). Most are now standard Kubernetes ecosystem concepts.

sections.title: Google — The Internet's Search Engine
sections.description:
GOOGLE
8.5B
Search queries per day
<200ms
Median search latency
100B+
Pages indexed
2M+
YouTube hours uploaded/day
GFS / Bigtable / MapReduce (2003–2006). The "Big Three" papers that defined distributed systems for a decade. GFS: 64MB chunks on commodity hardware, single master + chunk servers, write-once-append-many. Bigtable: sparse, distributed, persistent multidimensional sorted map — rows × columns × timestamps. MapReduce: split, map, shuffle/sort, reduce — automatic parallelism on thousands of machines. Apache Hadoop/HBase/Hive are direct ports of these papers.

Spanner (2012). Globally distributed relational database. Key innovation: TrueTime API — GPS + atomic clock hardware in every datacenter to get globally bounded clock uncertainty (±7ms). Used to implement external consistency (strong consistency across continents) without distributed locks. Supports SQL, ACID transactions, automatic sharding. Now available as Cloud Spanner.

Borg / Kubernetes (2014). Google ran their entire infrastructure on Borg (internal cluster manager) for 10+ years before open-sourcing the ideas as Kubernetes in 2014. Kubernetes now runs 5.6M+ clusters worldwide. Borg introduced: bin-packing containers on machines, declarative spec (desired state), health checking, graceful preemption.

PageRank & modern ranking. Original PageRank modeled the web as a Markov chain — probability a random surfer lands on your page. Modern Google Search uses a neural ranking model called MUM (Multitask Unified Model, 1000× more powerful than BERT) and a two-tower dense retrieval system for initial candidate recall.

TPUs (Tensor Processing Units). Google designed custom ASICs for ML inference/training. TPU v4 pods reach 1.1 EFLOPS (10¹⁸ FLOPs). AlphaGo, AlphaFold, Gemini, BERT were all trained on TPUs. Key design: systolic array matrix multiply unit — avoids DRAM reads by flowing data through compute units.

SRE & SLO culture. Google invented the SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) discipline. Core concepts: error budgets (100% reliability is never the goal), SLOs (Service Level Objectives), toil reduction, blameless postmortems. The SRE book (2016) is now a standard reference for ops culture at scale.

sections.title: Cross-Company: Technologies & Origins
sections.description:
Many foundational technologies were created by FAANG companies and open-sourced. Knowing the origin helps in system design interviews — you understand why a technology was built the way it was.
TechnologyOriginYearWhy built
HadoopYahoo (port of Google GFS/MR)2006Open-source MapReduce + HDFS
CassandraFacebook → Apache2008Inbox search, write-heavy, geo-distributed
KafkaLinkedIn → Apache2011Activity stream, high-throughput pub/sub log
Docker / GodotCloud / Google2009 / 2013Containers for dev; replace C++ internal tools
KubernetesGoogle (from Borg)2014Container orchestration at scale
ReactMeta (Facebook)2013Declarative UI, virtual DOM diffing
RocksDBMeta (from LevelDB)2013Write-heavy KV store, LSM tree
TensorFlowGoogle Brain2015ML research + production serving
gRPCGoogle (from Stubby)2015Efficient RPC with Protobuf & HTTP/2
GraphQLMeta (Facebook)2015Replace REST for mobile — fetch exactly what you need

sections.title: FAANG Interview: Numbers Worth Memorizing
sections.description:
When a system design interviewer asks "how would you handle X at Google scale?", having real numbers gives your answers credibility. These are the figures that come up most in senior engineer interviews.
Latency Numbers
L1 cache: ~1ns
L2 cache: ~4ns
RAM access: ~100ns
SSD random read: ~100µs
HDD random read: ~10ms
RTT same datacenter: ~500µs
RTT cross-region: ~100ms
RTT cross-continent: ~300ms
Storage Scale
1 char = 1 byte
1 avg tweet = ~300 bytes
1 photo (compressed) = ~300KB
1 min HD video = ~100MB
1B users × 1KB = 1TB
1B users × 1MB = 1PB
Twitter: ~400M tweets/day
Instagram: ~100M photos/day
Throughput Benchmarks
MySQL write: ~1K QPS
PostgreSQL: ~5K QPS
Redis: ~100K QPS
Kafka: ~1M msgs/sec
HTTP server: ~10K RPS
CDN edge: ~1M RPS
Nginx: ~50K concurrent
gRPC: ~5× faster than REST
Cost Rule of Thumb
SSD: ~$0.10/GB/month
HDD: ~$0.023/GB/month
S3: ~$0.023/GB/month
EC2 t3.medium: ~$30/month
Data egress: ~$0.09/GB
Bandwidth: ~$0.09/GB (AWS)
CDN delivery: ~$0.008/GB
GPU (A100, 1hr): ~$3
sections.codeBlock:
# Back-of-envelope: Twitter-scale tweet storage
# 400M tweets/day, store for 5 years

tweet_size_bytes = 300          # avg compressed tweet
tweets_per_day   = 400_000_000

storage_per_day  = tweet_size_bytes * tweets_per_day  # 120 GB/day
storage_5_years  = storage_per_day * 365 * 5          # 219 TB

# Add 3× replication
with_replication = storage_5_years * 3                 # 657 TB

# Read QPS: 200M DAU, avg 30 reads/day
read_qps = (200_000_000 * 30) // 86400                # ~70K QPS

# Write QPS: 400M writes/day
write_qps = 400_000_000 // 86400                       # ~4.6K QPS

print(f'Storage (5yr, 3× replica): {with_replication/1024:.1f} PB')
print(f'Peak read QPS (2× avg):    {read_qps * 2:,}')
print(f'Write QPS:                  {write_qps:,}')
# Storage: 0.6 PB  |  Reads: 140K QPS  |  Writes: 4.6K QPS

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-value Store (DeCandia et al., 2007) — Amazon's foundational distributed systems paper
  • TAO: Facebook's Distributed Data Store for the Social Graph (Bronson et al., 2013) — The graph database powering Meta's social infrastructure
  • Spanner: Google's Globally Distributed Database (Corbett et al., 2012) — Google's TrueTime-based globally distributed database
  • Netflix Tech Blog — Engineering culture, chaos engineering, and content delivery at internet scale
  • System Design Interview Repository — Curated system design resources with FAANG scale numbers
  • Meta Engineering Blog — Real-world infrastructure and architecture decisions at planetary scale
